They must have been known to them and to a large circle through the execution of these orders, and, of course, through the return of the wounded. The German people must have learned about them.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: In other words, it was only an opinion of yours, but in no way a fact based on personal observation?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: No, it was not. I personally never had anything to do with any SA leader. I never had anything to do with them, and I do not think any one of them knows me well.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Could you make a statement on this, that is whether the orders which were mentioned yesterday were given to the formations of the SA?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: Would you kindly formulate that question again?

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Could you make another statement as to whether the contents of these orders, which were discussed yesterday, were sent to the formations of the SA through official channels?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: No, not through official channels, but in the way I have previously indicated; in other words, members of the SA who were also in the Wehrmacht could see actually what happened out there, and when they came back they spoke about it, the same as anyone else. It was only in this connection . . .

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Is it known to you whether members of the SA had anything at all to do with the handling of prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: When members of the SA were in the Wehrmacht, yes.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Did you make any personal observations in this connection?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: No, I never said that. I said I had already talked about the SA.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: I asked you which leaders of the SA formations knew about them, and you answered that they should have known about them.

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: I said the leaders of these organizations came to know about them in this way.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: And today I ask you whether the individual formations of the SA had received these orders.

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: I can only repeat what I said yesterday, and I think I was very clear on the subject, in other words, how these orders were issued. I myself did not read these orders, but I know the effects they had.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: I can imagine myself how this happened, but I asked you whether you know anything about how these orders reached the SA?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: You do not know? Do you know anything from your own personal observations about members of the SA being employed for the supervision of prisoner-of-war camps?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: Yes, because from my personal observations, once when I was on my way to the Army Group North, I caught an SA man who was kicking a Russian prisoner of war and I pulled him up about it. I think that is mentioned somewhere in my records, and also an episode about an Arbeitsdienst man.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Did you report any of these incidents through the proper channels? Did you see to it that the leaders of this organization were informed about them?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: I reported it to my superior officer, or it was mentioned in my report on my visit either orally or in writing. There were discussions on this and similar incidents.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Have you got anything in your records?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Will you please submit it?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: I am looking it up. This is about the Arbeitsdienst man, this document.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: It is not about the SA man?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Then you cannot submit anything in answer to my question?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: I do not have it here. I would have to look it up.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Do you think you might find some records?

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: I would have to have an opportunity of going through the whole of the material which is in the hands of the American authorities to find this one.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: I will ask the Court that you be given this opportunity. I would also like to inquire whether you were ever able to observe that members of the SA whom you ascertained were employed on supervisory duties, ever took any measures which were in line with the orders against Soviet soldiers.

turns.speaker: LAHOUSEN
turns.text: No, not personally.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I would like to ask the Court for a fundamental ruling on whether the defendant also has the right personally to ask the witness questions. According to the German text of the Charter , Paragraph 16, I believe this is permissible.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal will consider the point you have raised and will let you know later.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The United States Prosecution would desire to be heard, I am sure, if there were any probability of that view being taken by the Tribunal.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Perhaps we had better hear you now, Mr. Justice Jackson.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, I think it is very clear that these provisions are mutually exclusive. Each defendant has the right to conduct his own defense or to have the assistance of counsel. Certainly this would become a performance rather than a trial if we go into that sort of thing. In framing this Charter, we anticipated the possibility that some of these defendants, being lawyers themselves, might conduct their own defenses. If they do so, of course they have all the privileges of counsel. If they avail themselves of the privileges of counsel, they are not, we submit, entitled to be heard in person.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I would like to point out once more that Paragraph 16 (e) , according to my opinion, speaks very clearly for my point of view. It says that the defendant has the right, either personally or through his counsel, to present evidence, and according to the German text it is clear that the defendant has the right to cross-examine any witness called by the Prosecution. According to the German text there reference can be made only to the defendant-with respect to terms as well as to the contents. In my opinion it is made clear that the defendant has the right to cross-examine any witness called by the Prosecution.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does any other German counsel, defendant's counsel, wish to cross-examine the witness?

turns.speaker: DR. ROBERT SERVATIUS (Counsel for Defendant Sauckel)
turns.text: I would only like to point out that in the written forms given to us by the Court, the defendant, as well as his counsel can make a motion. A place is left for two signatures on the questionnaire. I conclude, therefore, that the defendant himself has the right to speak on the floor.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What I asked was whether any other defendant's counsel wished to cross-examine the witness. [Herr Boehm approached the lectern]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What is it? Would you put the earphones on, please, unless you understand English. What is it you want to ask now? You have already cross-examined the witness.

turns.speaker: HERR BOHM
turns.text: Yes, I have cross-examined him, but he has given me to understand that he made a report about an incident which occurred during one of his visits of inspection, and that he has some written notes. As I am not yet able to release the witness, I should like to move that the Prosecution allow to be placed at the disposal of the witness any available notes or reports on the observations made by him at the time, so that he may find the evidence he wants.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I think you must conclude your cross-examination now.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Court thinks it would be better if you want to make any further application with reference to this witness, that you should make it in writing later.

turns.speaker: HERR BOEHM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Then, as no other defendant's counsel wishes to cross-examine the witness, the Tribunal will now retire for the purpose of considering the question raised by Dr. Stahmer as to whether a defendant has the right to cross-examine as well as his own counsel. [A recess was taken]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal has carefully considered the question raised by Dr. Stahmer, and it holds that defendants who are represented by counsel have not the right to cross-examine witnesses. They have the right to be called as witnesses themselves and to make a statement at the end of the Trial. Do the Prosecutors wish to ask any questions of this witness in re-examination?

turns.speaker: COLONEL JOHN HARLAN AMEN (Associate Trial Counsel for the United States)
turns.text: Just one question, your Lordship.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Let the witness come back here.

turns.speaker: THE MARSHAL (Colonel Charles W. Mays)
turns.text: He was taken away.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Taken away?

turns.speaker: THE MARSHAL
turns.text: That's right. He was taken away by some captain who brought him here for the Trial. They have sent after him now.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do you know how far he has been taken away?

turns.speaker: THE MARSHAL
turns.text: No, Sir, I do not. I will find out immediately.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Amen, are the questions that you wish to ask of sufficient importance for the Tribunal to wait for this witness or for him to be recalled on Monday?

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: I don't believe so, Your Lordship.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well then. The Tribunal will adjourn, and it will be understood that in the future no witness will be removed whilst he is under examination, from the precincts of this Court except on the orders of the Tribunal.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: I do not know how that happened Your Lordship, I understood he was still here. [The Tribunal adjourned until 3 December 1945 at 1000 hours]
